'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|qq=draft}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}}. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}}, it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|qq=draft}} (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|qq=draft}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|qq=draft}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft}}, a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the {{ill|Insei period|ja|院政|qq=draft}} onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' - Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. - Worn over {{ill|Sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft}} by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. - Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. - The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' - Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. - Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. - Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' - A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. - Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' - Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. - Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|qq=draft}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|qq=draft}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|qq=draft}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|qq=}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niiname-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōsai|qq=}} (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|qq=}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|qq=}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|qq=}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of {{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagura|qq=}}, a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the {{ill|Insei period|ja|院政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei period|qq=}} onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' - Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. - Worn over {{ill|Sokutai|ja|束帯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokutai|qq=}} by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. - Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. - The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' - Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. - Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. - Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' - A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. - Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' - Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. - Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachimangū|qq=}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōsai|qq=}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|ja|巫女|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miko|qq=}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabuki|qq=}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|小忌衣|version=92454623|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Omigoromo]] }}

1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:冬の季語
revision.text:
'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|qq=}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|qq=}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|qq=}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|qq=}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|qq=}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|qq=}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|qq=}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11460775]] Wikidata name: omigoromo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|qq=}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|qq=}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|qq=}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|qq=|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|qq=|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11460775]] Wikidata name: omigoromo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588671}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55535655}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

'''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11460775]] Wikidata name: omigoromo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588671}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55535655}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11460775]] Wikidata name: omigoromo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588671}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-Matsuri]], the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]] (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55535655}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of [[:en:Kagura|Kagura]], a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei period]] onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over [[:en:Sokutai|Sokutai]] by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern [[:en:Miko|Miko]] costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.

4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.

'''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=Sokutai}} by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.

3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|WD=Q1047139|lt=Miko}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]]
'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588671}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}, the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}} (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55535655}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}}, a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei period}} onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=Sokutai}} by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma Asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|WD=Q1047139|lt=Miko}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}

3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|WD=Q1047139|lt=Miko|1=Miko}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|小忌衣|version=92454623|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小忌衣]]
'''Omigoromo''' (小忌衣) is a traditional upper garment used in {{ill|Shinto rituals|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto rituals|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588671}} and other ceremonies since ancient times. Its origins trace back to the blue-dyed garments (青摺衣) mentioned in texts such as the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it was used by nobles and officials participating in major rituals such as the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}, the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}} (a special new harvest festival marking the accession of a new emperor), and the biannual rituals of {{ill|Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso (Sacred Meals)|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55535655}}. These rituals were performed as {{ill|Shinsai|ja|親祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the emperor personally conducted the ceremonies. During Kamimiso, participants did not wear the decorative heart leaves or shaded garlands on their crowns, as noted in sources like the {{ill|Saikyūki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikyūki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}}.
The name "Omigoromo" is derived from its primary use by individuals chosen as "Omi" (小忌 or 小斎) through divination for sacred service during rituals. Those not chosen were referred to as "Ōmi" (大忌), meaning "ordinary" participants without key roles. The term "忌" conveys a sense of sacredness.
Both men and women wore the Omigoromo over their traditional attire, with a red cord (sometimes red-black) draped over the right shoulder. However, performers of {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}}, a type of sacred dance, draped the cord over the left shoulder for practicality. The garment typically featured white silk or hemp fabric with simple floral, avian, or natural motifs painted using mountain indigo dye (山藍). Variants with and without sleeves existed.
== Types of Omigoromo == Based on regulations established from the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei period|1=Cloistered rule}} onward, the following types are described:
1. '''Shoshi Omigoromo (諸司小忌):''' *** Made of four panels (two for the body and one for each sleeve), with a straight collar and no side panels. *** Worn over {{ill|Sokutai|en|Sokutai|WD=Q1156828|lt=Sokutai|1=Sokutai}} by nobles and officials on the day of Shinsai. *** Typically made of hemp with powdered white coating (粉張), featuring indigo-dyed motifs like gentians and long-tailed birds. *** The red cord indicated Rank: gold-decorated for ministers, white motifs for court nobles, and simpler designs for lower-Ranked individuals.
2. '''Shi Omigoromo (私小忌):''' *** Single-panel design with long hems resembling hunting garments. *** Used by pre-assigned officials, such as priests and provincial governors, during major rituals. *** Featured unique motifs determined by tradition, such as chrysanthemums and flowing water.
3. '''Nyokei Omigoromo (如形小忌):''' *** A simplified version of the Shoshi Omigoromo, without sleeves. *** Worn mainly by lower-Ranking officials and staff during ceremonies.
4. '''Betchoku Omigoromo (別勅小忌):''' *** Used by close attendants of the emperor during rituals, independent of divination-based selection. *** Worn over court robes, featuring motifs like flowing water and ferns.
== Historical and Ritual Significance == The dye for Omigoromo was traditionally sourced from indigo plants growing at the {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} shrine. In dances like the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東遊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Songs|1=Azuma-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11528414}}, performers wore garments akin to Shi Omigoromo with a left-draped red cord.
== Modern Developments == During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, court and Shinto rituals saw a shift in ceremonial attire, with the Omigoromo largely replaced by the {{ill|Saifuku|ja|斎服|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saifuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the name and symbolic aspects persisted for use in specific contexts, such as the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.
Omigoromo motifs evolved to signify Rank, with imperial family members using pine and chrysanthemum patterns, while officials used plum and willow designs.
== Folklore and Influence == According to legend, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} wore the Omigoromo when emerging from her rock cave, a tale often cited as its origin. Historical references suggest its use since the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, with variations noted in ceremonial dances and traditional attire.
Omigoromo continues to inspire modern {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|WD=Q1047139|lt=Miko|1=Miko}} costumes and performances like the {{ill|Urayasu-no-mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu-no-mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}. Its legacy also appears in theatrical adaptations, such as {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, albeit in a stylized form unrelated to its original use.
